content: When you first implement a custom iterator in Rust using the Trait, you almost always start by wrapping your data in an Option and returning None or Some(value) based on whether you're done. That approach works fine for simple sequences like counting to ten, but it collapses under pressure when you need to maintain complex internal state across multiple calls to next. Imagine you are building a Fibonacci generator where each number depends on the previous two; if you simply store a Vec in your iterator structure and return None once exhausted, you end up with O(n) memory usage for every single element generated, which is unacceptable for streams that might run indefinitely or process millions of items. The real trap developers fall into is thinking they can just hold references to borrowed data inside their iterator closure; while this seems efficient, it introduces hidden costs when the iterator outlives its borrow scope or when you try to clone the state across threads without careful synchronization. Instead, embrace the ownership model fully by carrying mutable fields directly within your Iterator struct so that next() can update those fields in place without needing to allocate a new value on every iteration. For instance, rather than returning an Option<i32>, define a struct like MyFibonacci that holds two i32 variables representing the last two numbers computed and implements the standard Trait methods where next() simply checks if both values are still valid, computes the sum, stores it back into the first variable, shifts the current value to the second slot, and returns the result. This pattern eliminates unnecessary heap allocations because you never allocate a new boxed option or clone large structures; you just move the computed value out of the iterator while keeping the state alive for the next call. Performance-wise, this is significantly better than using lazy evaluation with closures that capture references to external vectors, especially when dealing with recursive algorithms like tree traversals where the depth determines how much state must be preserved between nodes. If you were implementing a countdown timer or a post-order traversal of a binary tree, carrying mutable counters and pointers directly in your iterator ensures that each step is O(1) time complexity regardless of total sequence length, avoiding the stack overflow risks associated with naive recursion or the memory bloat of storing every intermediate result. The key insight is that custom iterators shouldn't just be wrappers around data; they should be active participants in the computation by mutating their own internal fields to track progress, which gives you precise control over the lifecycle and content of each iteration without relying on external borrow rules that often trip up experienced developers when moving between threads or async contexts.
